parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Ebenezer Devotion was the pastor who encouraged Joseph Huntington's late start in college preparation. Joseph then married his daughter Hannah.
Rev. Ebenezer Devotion was the pastor who encouraged Joseph Huntington's late start in college preparation. Joseph then married his daughter Hannah.

death.cause: Complication of diseases per contemporary account

education.notes: Yale College B.A. 1762. Entered at age 23 after his father had destined him for the clothier trade against his own wishes; his pastor Rev. Ebenezer Devotion encouraged the late preparation. Graduated honorably.

career.notes: Installed June 29, 1763 — within one year of his Yale graduation. Continued as pastor until his death thirty-one years later. Under his ministry the parish built the best meeting house in the region for $5,000 — services at his ordination were held in the open air either because the existing meeting house was too small or too dilapidated.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1762; D.D. honoris causa 1780; Trustee 1780-1788.

notable.notable_blurb: Coventry minister who posthumously published Universalism

cross_connections.display_label: Tutored Nathan Hale at Coventry for Yale entrance

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter burned most copies of his Universalist treatise
narrative_blocks.body: 'Calvinism Improved' — his posthumous 1796 treatise defending universal salvation and denying eternal punishment — was doomed to limited circulation because one of his daughters consigned the greater part of the edition to the flames. Sprague's Annals of the American Pulpit notes 'so effectual was the suppression of the work that it is now almost impossible to find a copy.'

narrative_blocks.header: Son from first marriage became Ohio Governor
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son with Hannah Devotion — Samuel H. Huntington — was orphaned at age six when Hannah died in 1771 and was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington, the Connecticut Governor and Declaration signer. The boy grew up to attend Dartmouth and Yale, settled Cleveland in 1801, and became the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 — half-brother to the nine children Joseph would later have with second wife Elizabeth Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Tutored Nathan Hale of Coventry for Yale
narrative_blocks.body: He prepared Nathan Hale (1755-1776) of Coventry for Yale before Hale entered the college in 1769 and graduated 1773. Hale was hanged at New York September 22, 1776 — 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country.' Both Huntington and Hale are buried at the Nathan Hale Cemetery in Coventry.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost succeeded Wheelock as Dartmouth president, 1780
narrative_blocks.body: After the death of Rev Eleazar Wheelock — Dartmouth's first president — communications were made to Huntington that gave him reason to expect election as Wheelock's successor. He actually made the journey to Hanover NH before declining. Yale conferred a D.D. honoris causa on him that same year, and he was elected a Yale Trustee 1780-1788.

narrative_blocks.header: Preached Universalism quietly for two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Old parishioners later claimed 'Dr Huntington preached good Universalism for twenty years, but in such a way that he was not suspected, except by those who relished it.' His preface to Calvinism Improved acknowledged the work 'contained a small part of a system of divinity which the author has been meditating more than twenty years' — his Coventry parish never knew their pastor's actual theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Open-air ordination at decayed Coventry meeting house
narrative_blocks.body: Installed June 29, 1763 as pastor of the First Church of Coventry, where the previous meeting house had been suffered to go to decay. The ordination services were held in the open air, whether because the building was too small or too dilapidated to be safe never made clear. Within a few years he led the parish to build the best house of public worship in the region at a cost of five thousand dollars.

death.cause: Killed in accident while supervising road construction to his estate at Painesville.

parents.father_research_notes: Biological father Rev Joseph Huntington (Y00008). Orphaned as young child after mother's death 1771 and likely shortly after.
parents.mother_research_notes: Biological mother Hannah Devotion (Y00009) died 1771 when Samuel was age 6. Adopted by uncle Samuel Huntington Sr (Y00012), Joseph's brother and Declaration signer.

education.notes: Dartmouth College through junior year; transferred to Yale before completing degree at Dartmouth.

career.notes: Associate Justice 1803; succeeded Return J. Meigs Jr as Chief Justice 1804. Resigned 1808 upon election as Governor.

3rd Governor of Ohio Dec 12, 1808 - Dec 8, 1810. Democratic-Republican. Stormy tenure with controversy over judicial impeachments for upholding judicial review (Huntington would have been impeached as well had he not been elected governor); state capital moved from Zanesville to Chillicothe; Tiffin Resolution terminated terms of all sitting judges; passage of Ohio's 'Blue Laws'; approaching war with Great Britain. Did not stand for reelection. First Ohio governor born in the United States.
Ran for US Senate 1810; lost to Thomas Worthington (later Ohio's 6th governor).

military_service.unit: Western Reserve state militia troops, Northwest Territory
military_service.notes: Appointed Lieutenant Colonel of state militia troops in the Western Reserve by Territorial Governor Arthur St. Clair (also appointed Justice of the Peace by St. Clair).
Appointed Paymaster and Colonel in the United States Army by Gen William Henry Harrison in 1813.

institutions.institution_blurb: Dartmouth through junior year before transferring to Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed supervising road construction at Painesville, 1817
narrative_blocks.body: On June 8, 1817 he was killed in an accident while supervising construction on a road leading to his estate at Painesville OH. He was fifty-one and had just finished his Ohio House term five years earlier. His wife Hannah followed him seventeen months later; both lie at Evergreen Cemetery in Painesville.

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted by uncle the Declaration signer
narrative_blocks.body: Orphaned as a young child after his mother Hannah Devotion died in 1771, he was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington Sr — his biological father Joseph's brother, Declaration of Independence signer, and four years later President of the Continental Congress. The adoption made him son of two of the most prominent men in late-eighteenth-century Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded village of Fairport on Lake Erie, 1812
narrative_blocks.body: After losing the 1810 US Senate race to Thomas Worthington and operating a Newburgh grist mill for a few years, he moved to Painesville Township and helped found the village of Fairport on Lake Erie in 1812. The Fairport harbor and its surrounding settlement became one of the early Lake Erie port communities of northeast Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: Ohio Supreme Court Chief Justice then 3rd Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Ohio Supreme Court Associate Justice April 1803 (the year Ohio gained statehood); became Chief Justice 1804 when Return J Meigs Jr resigned. Elected 3rd Governor of Ohio 1808 — the first Ohio governor born in the United States. His stormy single term saw judicial impeachments, the capital's move to Chillicothe, and passage of Ohio's Blue Laws.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Samuel Huntington (1731-1796) — Connecticut Governor 1786-1796 and signer of the Declaration of Independence — who would later adopt him after his mother's 1771 death. The uncle is not built as a separate tree entry (out of scope; he has no direct Hooker- or Talcott-line connections of his own).

parents.father_research_notes: Parents not researched. Shared Huntington surname + Norwichtown (Samuel Huntington Sr's town) strongly suggests cousin marriage; possibly daughter of one of Samuel Sr's Huntington brothers.
